summary: Jeff Atwood shares the surprising origin of the Windows 95 startup sound, revealing it was composed by avant-garde musician Brian Eno. The post highlights Eno's creative process, where strict constraints (3.25 seconds, specific emotional qualities) led to unexpected artistic breakthroughs. Eno created 84 variations and found the exercise so valuable that it unlocked his own stalled creative work. Atwood connects this to the broader Windows 95 media phenomenon and notes the anticipation around the upcoming Windows Vista release.
key_points: The iconic Windows 95 startup sound was composed by Brian Eno, a famous avant-garde electronic musician
Eno was creatively stuck when Microsoft approached him, and he found the specific constraints liberating rather than limiting
He created 84 different pieces while working on the 3.25-second sound, becoming 'sensitive to microseconds'
The tight constraints ultimately broke a creative logjam in Eno's own longer-form musical work
Windows 95 had a massive marketing campaign including the Rolling Stones' 'Start Me Up'
money_quotes: I really appreciated someone coming along and saying, 'Here's a specific problem – solve it.'

The thing from the agency said, 'We want a piece of music that is inspiring, universal, blah-blah, da-da-da, optimistic, futuristic, sentimental, emotional,' this whole list of adjectives, and then at the bottom it said 'and it must be 3 1/4 seconds long.'
I thought this was so funny and an amazing thought to actually try to make a little piece of music. It's like making a tiny little jewel.
I was so sensitive to microseconds at the end of this that it really broke a logjam in my own work.
Then when I'd finished that and I went back to working with pieces that were like three minutes long, it seemed like oceans of time.
key_insight: Creative constraints, even absurdly tight ones, can paradoxically unlock innovation and break through creative blocks.
